chapters.title: It’s not what you think
chapters.text:
Memory is like a spouse with whom you've shared countless happy, sad, and cringeworthy moments, along with inside jokes and songs that make you go, "Oh, remember when…"

Yet, you’ve sadly started noticing how you’ve grown apart lately. They forgot your birthday this year and were overall less present than before. Should you let the relationship go or try to fix it?

Don’t expect your memory to record everything; focus on remembering what’s important to you.

Unfortunately, many people experience this downward spiral regarding memory. Under the spell of anxiety and overthinking, they start frantically googling for the signs of Alzheimer’s after forgetting an actor's name or where they parked their car. But just like any relationship, suspicion only breeds discontent.

Knowledge is the best therapy if you're worried about your memory skills. Instead of unfounded fears, you need science-backed information to rebuild a harmonious relationship with your memory. In other words, you must understand how it ticks.

Come along and learn how your brain remembers and forgets to equip yourself for a lifelong, mentally satisfying journey!

chapters.title: How to cook a memory
chapters.text:
If creating memories is cooking, then your hippocampus — your brain's memory center — is a five-Michelin-star chef. It can whip up anything and has a slightly better aptitude for specific dishes or cuisines. For instance, you might be better at remembering numbers than names — no big deal.

Just like a great cook can create an unforgettable meal out of seemingly mismatched ingredients, your brain can weave unrelated information into a cohesive memory you’ll carry throughout your lifetime.

Sounds like magic, right? It is mesmerizing, yet behind every magic trick is a clear, step-by-step process refined to perfection. So, if you’re ready, let’s demystify memory creation.

Memory is like a ball of yarn. Once you’ve found an association, you can unravel an entire story from memory.

Before information becomes a memory in your brain, it goes through four stages: encoding, consolidation, storage, and retrieval.

To illustrate, imagine you’re in Paris with your significant other. Your hippocampus rolls up its sleeves and starts cooking:

• Encoding: Your brain gathers what you’ve sensed and paid attention to — sounds, smells, context, words, pictures, and emotions and “writes them down” in a language of neuronal connections. You enjoyed the weather, felt romantic, bought a new perfume, admired the beautiful architecture, and savored fresh macarons.
• Consolidation: Then, your brain creates a storyline of associations from previously unconnected pieces of information. It combines the sun, kisses, the Eiffel Tower, the scent, and macarons into a memory called, for instance, “Our Love in Paris.”
• Storage: Your neuronal landscape changes to make room for newly formed connections, creating new, lasting links and bonds. Now, your days in Paris have a place of honor in your memory.
• Retrieval: The last step is activating these connections, allowing you to recall previously learned information or experiences. In that case, smelling the perfume you wore during your trip will remind you of your lovely time in sunny France.

Voilà! Savor your precious memories.

chapters.title: What’s in it for me?
chapters.text:
You know by now that your hippocampus is the big shot when it comes to memory. Need to remember something? Better call hippocampus.

As an important figure, it doesn’t let just anybody into its domain — information needs to be of some value to make its way to your memory and stay there. Yet, there are tips to help you memorize things your brain may initially ignore.

Pay attention: Your brain registers what you focus on and ignores everything else. So, if you can't find your keys, don't blame your memory for not doing its job correctly — make sure to pay closer attention next time. Unfortunately, noticing and concentrating on something usually takes conscious effort. Your brain won’t do that by default; it's too cool for that.

Don’t stress when you can’t remember something. Your memory works best when you are relaxed and appreciated.

Visualize: Creating imagery around what you want to retain will help you wrap it into more neural connections and make memory formation easier. Add pictures, use highlighters, and construct visual boards to remember something. Just know that your hippocampus loves with its “eyes.”

Add meaning: Your brain’s too busy to entertain itself with empty information. So, to “sell” new knowledge to your hippocampus, season it with significance — add emotions, storylines, or characters. Whatever you want to remember, make it mean something to you.

Create novelty: Overwhelmed by data, your brain ignores ho-hum, same-as-always things. Why remember last night’s dinner if it was the same as the week before? Fair enough, beloved brain. Thus, add diversity and step out of your routines to start remembering more.

Be positive: It might sound cheesy, but your attitude decides a lot. Treat your memory as your dear, dedicated friend rather than an irresponsible employee. Focus on pleasant aspects of its commitment to you while taking steps to improve its performance.

Like people, your memory will function better if it has high self-esteem. ~ Lisa Genova

Repeat: If memory had a love language, it would be repetition. Through practice, self-quizzing, and intentional recollection, you strengthen your newly built neuronal connections.

chapters.title: Let your hands do the work
chapters.text:
Can you explain how you walk? Which leg goes first, which follows? Which muscle groups do you use? Do you land on your heel or the outer part of your sole?

Thinking about all those aspects would’ve made you slow and clumsy. But that doesn’t mean you can’t walk. Walking just happens while enjoying the sunny weather and thinking about more important matters during your stroll in the park.

That’s muscle memory in action. You probably learned how to walk when you were around one year old: your brain saved these complicated movements, organized them into one activity, and now retrieves this memory unconsciously whenever you’re headed somewhere on foot.

In other words, you don't have to think about the choreography of walking while you walk: your brain uses muscle memory to free up mental space for more exciting things. Isn't that awesome? Otherwise, you could have gone shopping and never got your groceries, as you were too busy deciding which leg to move next.

Like a CEO, your brain delegates motor skills to muscle memory without getting consciously involved.

Even more exciting is that muscle memory has nothing to do with the hippocampus. Memory about how to do physical tasks resides in a different part of the brain called the basal ganglia, which makes remembering different. Here, you learn by doing.

Unfortunately, you can't just read how to play the piano and then flawlessly perform something from Rakhmaninov. You need to sit and practice those notes repeatedly until the moves flow seamlessly from your fingertips, creating a musical experience.

So, to consolidate new motor skills, you must dedicate yourself to rehearsing. After focused practice, the memory of how to perform a task — like playing a musical piece — gets saved in your motor cortex, and you can activate it effortlessly. That’s why maestros can close their eyes and be in the moment while performing; muscle memory has their back.

chapters.title: You lie more than you know
chapters.text:
It’s Thanksgiving. The whole family has gathered around the turkey, indulging in homemade food and catching up. Then someone exclaims, “Oh, remember that time when…” and starts telling a crazy story you’ve heard at least 113 times already.

Interestingly, every time the tale is told, it gets adorned with new wild details, turning into more of a blockbuster from year to year. You don’t object, but still — why lie for the sake of a better story? Well, your relatives probably aren’t lying. Their memory of what happened is wrong, just like yours.

Your episodic memory, responsible for personal experiences, is highly fragile and fallible.

Whenever you retrieve an experience, imagine your brain opening a Google document with this memory, going through it, making some adjustments due to your biases or mood, and then pressing “save.” Your recollection of what happened has changed irrevocably. And that happens every time you recall any story from your past.

By sharing experiences, people don’t simply convey information. Their stories show what they focus on.

If you are now scared to tell others your stories for fear of distorting them, relax. Those memories were incomplete from the get-go.

Your brain is incredibly biased when registering something: you focus on the things you consider valuable and let the less essential things slip away. That's why two people can witness the same incident and tell different testimonies. They’re not necessarily lying. Their episodic memory is playing tricks.

Moreover, your episodic memory is vulnerable to external influences whenever you activate it. For example, if you’re reminiscing on a story with a friend, they can innocuously add a little detail like “You cried at that point,” you’re highly likely to believe it — although you didn’t cry.

That said, avoid taking your memory of what happened at face value. Perhaps the next time your story doesn't perfectly align with someone else's, let it go. You're both probably wrong.

Did you know? Eyewitness mistakes have resulted in 70% of wrongful convictions in the United States. Luckily, many were overturned due to DNA evidence.

chapters.title: It’s not what you think
chapters.text:
If you forgot where you put your phone — even if that made you run late twice this week — don’t beat yourself up over it. More importantly, try not to take it as a sign of Alzheimer's. You likely just paid little attention to it, which happens to the best of us. But if you don’t remember having a phone, that’s the time to ring the alarm.

While still exploring the cause of Alzheimer’s, most neuroscientists believe that it results from a protein called amyloid beta that creates breaches in the synapses — places where neurons connect. The process begins in the hippocampus, impairing the ability to create new memories.

So, while forgetting to buy something or call someone is normal, people with Alzheimer’s would not be able to remember that they saw you today because their hippocampus failed to register that encounter. See the difference?

When operating optimally, your memory doesn’t remember everything. So, forgetting is good for you.

Although the word “Alzheimer’s” evokes a sense of helplessness in many, there are ways to prevent this disease:

• Mediterranean diet: People following the Mediterranean diet cut their risk of developing Alzheimer's by 30-50%. So, try to eat more leafy greens, omega-3-rich foods, nuts, fish, and colorful berries to give your brain some love and care.
• Sleep: Sleep deprivation skyrockets your risk of Alzheimer's. Yes, it's a tale as old as time, but 7-8 hours of daily sleep are crucial for your brain health.
• Exercise: Whatever is good for your cardiovascular system is suitable for your brain and keeps you away from Alzheimer’s. Add running, cycling, and walking into your daily routine.
• Learn new things: Putting new information into your brain creates new neuronal connections, which can stop the runaway train of Alzheimer’s disease. Start a language course, attend dance classes, or read a new book.

You are more than what you can remember. ~ Lisa Genova

Despite all the damage Alzheimer’s does to the brain, it can’t destroy love. So, even when people can’t remember the names of their beloved, they still remember how they made them feel. And that gives hope.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
If you don't know whether your memory is doing a good or a bad job for you, it’s best to reserve judgment. Your memory is capable of impressive accomplishments, yet sometimes, it fails you in the most basic situations. Now that you know how it works, you can boost it.

Moreover, use your knowledge about memory to be more compassionate toward yourself and others. Forgetting is a typical human experience. And sometimes you need to cut yourself some slack and Google the name of that actor you can’t quite remember.

Try this

• Keep a journal and write down things that happened to you during the day.
• Add visual elements to your studies or work, like illustrations, highlights, or infographics.
• Self-quiz yourself after learning something new.
• Add aerobic training to your routine.
• Introduce a new skill — join a language course or an art class.
